Description: Optimal average power for Tungsten oxide and contaminant removal
Near-IR wavelength optimized for Tungsten absorption and thermal processing
Focused spot size for precise cleaning resolution
High repetition rate for efficient cleaning coverage
Minimum fluence required for effective Tungsten oxide removal
Nanosecond pulse duration for controlled ablation of surface contaminants
Optimal scanning speed for uniform cleaning coverage
Recommended number of passes for complete contaminant removal
Optimal beam overlap for uniform cleaning without thermal damage

Typical range for industrial 1064 nm fiber lasers used in metal cleaning; starting at 200 W balances efficiency and safety for reflective Tungsten to avoid excessive reflection losses. Max safe below substrate melting threshold (~3422°C). Source: IPG Photonics YLR series specs and studies on laser cleaning of refractory metals (e.g., J. Laser Appl. 2018).

Effective range for contamination removal on high-conductivity metals like Tungsten; 2000 mm/s recommended for optimal balance of cleaning rate and fluence control. Slower for heavy contamination (e.g., oxides), faster for light surface prep. Source: Industry standards from Laserax and Adapt Laser Systems manuals.

Nanosecond regime typical for industrial cleaning to ablate contaminants without deep penetration; 50 ns recommended for minimal heat-affected zone (HAZ) on Tungsten due to its high thermal conductivity. Picosecond (ps) or femtosecond (fs) for ultra-precision but less common industrially. Source: Laser physics research in Opt. Express (2015) on pulse duration effects in metal ablation.

Standard for pulsed 1064 nm lasers in cleaning; 30 kHz recommended for Tungsten to achieve fast processing while limiting heat accumulation from its reflectivity. Higher rates increase speed but risk thermal buildup. Source: Academic literature, e.g., Appl. Surf. Sci. (2020) on repetition rate optimization for refractory metals.

Fluence range for contaminant ablation on Tungsten; damage threshold ~2-4 J/cm² at 1064 nm due to high reflectivity (~60%). 1.5 J/cm² recommended for safe oxide/carbon removal without substrate damage. Source: Ablation threshold studies in J. Phys. D: Appl. Phys. (2017) and fusion reactor cleaning papers (e.g., Nucl. Fusion 2019).

Typical beam diameter for precise industrial cleaning; 200 μm recommended for Tungsten to focus energy on contaminants while covering large areas efficiently. Smaller for fine details, larger for broad prep. Source: Equipment specs from Coherent and Trumpf laser cleaning systems.

Usually 1-3 passes for complete removal on metals; 2 passes recommended for Tungsten to ensure uniform cleaning of stubborn contaminants like tungsten oxides without overexposure. Multi-pass preferred over single high-fluence pass to minimize damage. Source: Industry case studies in laser cleaning of fusion components (e.g., J. Nucl. Mater. 2021).

Standard for uniform coverage in scanned laser cleaning; 50% recommended for Tungsten to avoid gaps in high-reflectivity surfaces while preventing excessive heat overlap. Higher (60-80%) for heavy contamination. Source: Process optimization studies in Surf. Coat. Technol. (2019).
